summaries.Economics: The main elements of the FSM economy vary from state to state and include subsistence farming, local fishing, lease of fishing rights to FSM waters, servicing of fishing fleets, government employment, and tourism. Tourism is considered to have potential for near-term income generation due to the natural and marine beauty of the islands, though limitations in air transportation, land-use issues, and competition with other island destinations closer to tourist markets represent challenges to developing larger-scale tourism investment. The FSM considers the ocean to be the country's most important resource, earning $18-24 million annually in recent years through licensing fees paid by foreign vessels for tuna fishing in the FSM's Exclusive Economic Zone. Rising sea levels are identified as a threat to infrastructure and local economies across Pacific Island jurisdictions, exacerbating existing inequities, with livelihoods and economies specifically cited as areas of concern alongside the built environment and decarbonization efforts.
summaries.Time zone: Howland and Baker Islands operate on a UTC-12:00 time offset, designated as Nautical Letter Y (Yankee), placing them among the United States Minor Outlying Islands in this time zone. This UTC-12:00 designation means that for any given calendar date, Howland and Baker Islands represent the latest place on Earth where that date remains valid, situating them in the IDLW (International Date Line West) time zone on the Western Hemisphere side of the International Date Line. This distinction makes Howland and Baker Islands the reference point for the "Anywhere on Earth" (AoE) calendar designation, which is used to specify deadlines that expire only when the date has passed at every location on Earth, with these two islands being the final locations where any given date remains active before rolling over globally.
summaries.History: Howland Island was first sighted in 1822 by George Worth, a Nantucket whaling-ship captain, who named it Worth Island, and in 1842 another American whaling ship visited the island, renaming it Howland Island after that ship's lookout. In 1791, Edmund Fanning, an American sailor, is also noted as having discovered the island, marking its entrance into recorded history. The United States claimed Howland in 1857, along with nearby Baker Island, under the Guano Act of 1856. Prior to 1890, organic nitrate (guano) was mined from the island by both the United States and the British, though the atoll's guano deposits are now exhausted. The island was colonized from Hawaii in 1935. In 1937, famous pilot Amelia Earhart attempted to land there during her around-the-world flight but never made it, adding an enduring mystery to the island's story. During World War II, the island served as a lookout point for the U.S. military. Today, Howland Island is part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system and the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, with no permanent residents and very limited human presence.
summaries.National Wildlife Refuge: Howland Island, a coral atoll and unincorporated territory of the United States located in the southwestern Pacific Ocean approximately 1,650 miles southwest of Honolulu, is currently part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system, providing nesting areas and forage for a variety of birds and marine wildlife, including several species of migratory seabirds and shorebirds as well as threatened and endangered sea turtles. The island was designated a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and was also designated part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument in 2009, which helps safeguard marine resources in the area. The island is not used for economic activities such as farming or industry due to its status as a nature reserve, and instead serves an essential role for science and ecology, with researchers visiting to study its wildlife and environment, helping to protect coral reefs and unique bird species. The tiny island, measuring 1.6 km², is composed of coral fragments and is surrounded by an active fringing reef, and astronauts aboard the International Space Station have photographed the reef as part of a global mapping and monitoring program, with high-resolution images used to update geographic maps of reefs, assess reef ecosystem health, and calculate bathymetry of the surrounding ocean bottom.
summaries.Earhart Light: The Earhart Light is a small lighthouse built in 1937 on Howland Island, a 450-acre uninhabited coral island located in an extremely remote part of the Pacific Ocean, almost halfway between Hawaii and Australia. It was constructed in anticipation of Amelia Earhart's scheduled refueling stop on the island during her 1937 transpacific flight, though Earhart never reached the island and is thought to have crashed in the waters just north of Howland Island. During World War II, on January 31st, 1941, a Japanese submarine fired 50 shells into the settlement on Howland Island, rendering the Amelia Earhart light beacon useless, and the station has been inactive since 1942, with the structure now considered a "day beacon." Brief visits have since been made to the island to restore the light beacon and to add the names of two colonists lost during the Japanese attack. The beacon remains on Howland Island today, which has been a National Wildlife Refuge under the jurisdiction of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service since 1974, and the island has remained uninhabited by humans except for brief scientific visits for more than 70 years.
summaries.Image gallery: The gallery of Howland Island includes a map of Howland Island and an image titled ISS010-E-09287, photographed on 3 December 2004 by an Expedition 10 crewmember aboard the International Space Station (ISS), sourced from the JSC Gateway to Astronaut Photography of Earth and credited to the Earth Science and Remote Sensing Unit at Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center. The image was captured using a Kodak DCS760c Electronic Still Camera with an 800 mm focal length from an altitude of 192 nautical miles (356 km), at a look angle of 41 degrees in the SW direction, with a sun azimuth of 115° and sun elevation of 19°, with approximately 10% cloud coverage. The photograph depicts Howland Island, a United States possession in the north Pacific between Australia and the Hawaiian Islands, showing the island composed of coral fragments surrounded by an active fringing reef, with white breakers encircling the island indicating the reef's position, and is used as part of a global reef mapping and monitoring program to update geographic maps, assess reef ecosystem health, and calculate bathymetry of the surrounding ocean bottom.
